title: Pathways to Recovery for Pregnant & Parenting Women (PPW) in Northern Arizona: A Needs Assessment
authors_full: Matt Martin, Jeremiah Kaplan, Swetha Ganesan, Nina Russin, William Riley
authors_short: Matt Martin Jeremiah Kaplan Swetha Ganesan Nina Russin William Riley

summary.hook: This poster presents a needs assessment of the care ecosystem for pregnant and parenting women (PPW) with substance use disorder (SUD) in Northern Arizona, described as...
summary.summary_30s: This poster presents a needs assessment of the care ecosystem for pregnant and parenting women (PPW) with substance use disorder (SUD) in Northern Arizona, described as compassionate but structurally fragile.

talktrack_2m.what_this_is_about: This poster presents a needs assessment of the care ecosystem for pregnant and parenting women (PPW) with substance use disorder (SUD) in Northern Arizona, described...
talktrack_2m.what_they_did: Qualitative needs assessment (semi-structured interviews; rapid qualitative analysis; cross-case synthesis).. Participants n=22; 17 interviews
talktrack_2m.what_they_found: The “core system problem” is fragmentation; relationships (not infrastructure) currently hold the system together.
talktrack_2m.why_it_matters: Practice/operations: Standardize screening and warm handoffs (48–72 hours) and assign navigators/peers as single contact points.
talktrack_2m.one_smart_follow_up: When you describe the ecosystem as “compassionate but structurally fragile,” what specific infrastructure elements are missing or underdeveloped?

takeaways: The “core system problem” is fragmentation; relationships (not infrastructure) currently hold the system together.
Housing, peers, and cultural responsiveness drive engagement more than any single clinical service.
Identification and engagement rely on trust and peer outreach; screening protocols are inconsistent; fear/stigma (e.g., fear of child removal) limits disclosure.
